answer:
For a healthy 65-year-old, the current evidence does not support routine aspirin use for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease due to the increased bleeding risk [1]. The American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association (ACC/AHA) guideline recommends that aspirin for primary prevention be considered on an individual basis in high-risk adults aged 40-70 years after a risk-benefit discussion with their clinician [2].

In contrast, high-intensity statin therapy is recommended for patients with clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and for primary prevention in adults 40-75 years with LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL or diabetes with additional risk factors [3].

The final answer is: Aspirin use for primary prevention in healthy 65-year-olds is not routinely recommended due to bleeding risk. Exceptions may be considered on an individual basis in high-risk adults aged 40-70 years after a risk-benefit discussion with their clinician.

[1] Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease, ACC/AHA, 2019

[2] Management of Blood Cholesterol, ACC/AHA, 2018

[3] Management of Blood Cholesterol, ACC/AHA, 2018

arguments.query: aspirin for primary prevention in healthy 65-year-olds

result.query: aspirin for primary prevention in healthy 65-year-olds

results.content: Aspirin for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease is not routinely recommended due to bleeding risk, except in select high-risk adults 40-70 years after a risk-benefit discussion with their clinician.
results.source: ACC/AHA Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease Guideline
results.citation: Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease, ACC/AHA, 2019

results.content: High-intensity statin therapy is recommended for patients with clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and for primary prevention in adults 40-75 years with LDL-C >=190 mg/dL or diabetes with additional risk factors.
For outpatient community-acquired pneumonia in healthy adults, amoxicillin or doxycycline is recommended. For inpatients, beta-lactam plus macrolide combination therapy is standard.

Low-dose aspirin starting at 12-28 weeks of gestation is recommended for patients at high risk of preeclampsia. Magnesium sulfate is recommended for seizure prophylaxis in patients with severe features of preeclampsia.

result.disclaimer: Evidence excerpts for professional review; not a diagnosis.
